title: Thermodynamic Irreversibility of Training Algorithms
authors: Liu Ziyin, Yuanjie Ren, Adam Levine, Isaac Chuang
abstract: The training algorithms for AI systems all introduce far-from-equilibrium dynamical processes, and understanding the irreversibility of these algorithms is a fundamental step towards understanding the learning dynamics of modern AI systems. In this work, we establish a general framework for defining and analyzing the irreversibility of training algorithms. We show that four different ways to characterize the irreversibility of dynamical processes are equivalent to leading order in the step size $η$: numerical backward error $φ_{\rm DE}$, time-renormalized correction $φ_{\rm TR}$, microscopic time reversal asymmetry $φ_{\rm TA}$, and the (regularized) stochastic-thermodynamic entropy production $φ_{\rm ST}$. The irreversibility gives rise to a time-reversal-symmetry-breaking emergent force that generically breaks non-isometric continuous reparametrization symmetries, preserves orthogonal symmetries, and leads to a universal preference for those learning trajectories that minimize the entropy production rate.
summary: This paper establishes that different ways of measuring the irreversibility of AI training algorithms are mathematically equivalent at small learning rates. It demonstrates that the error introduced by discrete training steps creates an emergent force that pushes models toward more stable and robust regions of the loss landscape.
main_problem: The paper aims to characterize the thermodynamic irreversibility of deep learning training algorithms and understand how discretization errors act as an emergent force in the optimization landscape.
main_result: The authors prove that four different measures of irreversibility (discretization error, time-renormalization, time-reversal asymmetry, and entropy production) are equivalent to leading order in the learning rate. They also show that this irreversibility generates an emergent force that drives training toward stable, low-fluctuation regions.
method: The work uses backward error analysis, renormalization group (RG) approaches to time-axis coarse-graining, and stochastic thermodynamics. Numerical validation is performed using Transformers, RNNs, and linear perceptrons.
model_or_system: The system is modeled as a discrete-time stochastic Langevin process for neural network parameters, where the update rule is defined by a vector field (e.g., SGD or Adam) with a learning rate eta.
key_observables: Discretization error potential (phi_DE), time-reversal asymmetry (phi_TA), stochastic-thermodynamic entropy production (phi_ST), and update energy (eU_s).
important_parameters: Learning rate (eta), noise scale (sigma), and the Jacobian of the update field (J_U).
main_assumptions: The update field is assumed to be smooth (C2 or C3) and possess a symmetric Jacobian (J_U = J_U^T). The analysis focuses on the small learning rate limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the qualitative and quantitative agreement between the four irreversibility potentials at small learning rates. Figure 2 illustrates 'anomalous fluctuations' in Transformers, where larger learning rates lead to lower update fluctuations.
paper_structure: The paper establishes a theoretical framework for irreversibility, proves the equivalence of four different metrics using backward error analysis and RG, analyzes the symmetry-breaking properties of the resulting emergent force, and provides numerical experiments on various neural architectures to validate the theory.
why_interesting: The paper provides a rigorous mapping between the discrete-time optimization of neural networks and non-equilibrium stochastic thermodynamics, offering a way to treat learning dynamics as a driven particle system with emergent potentials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. All four potentials agree with each other at a small learning rate. As the learning rates increase, they start to deviate, but still have the qualitative agreement that a higher learning rate corresponds to a higher potential, signaling that they are all qualitatively good metrics of irreversibility of the training process.

FIG. 2. Anomalous fluctuation effect in transformer training dynamics. There is a qualitative distinction between the effect of changing the learning rate in a simple linear model (perceptron) and in nonlinear models such as transformers. In linear models, the classical optimization theory predicts an increase in the fluctuation of the gradient due to increased instability [22] – because there is a single local minimum, and so it is impossible for the model to move to a different, low-fluctuation model. In sharp contrast, the nonlinear-model experiments reported here show lower update fluctuation at larger learning rate, in agreement with the prediction of the irreversibility theory.
